Go To Spain

April 21 – 28, 2026

Jen & David Go To Spain

April 21 – 28, 2026 · Madrid & Seville

Your Trip at a Glance

8 days · 2 cities · 1 unforgettable trip

1 Tuesday, April 21 Madrid
Arrival
Private Transfer to Hotel Fenix Gran Meliá
Your driver will meet you at the airport. Hotel is at Hermosilla 2, right off Plaza de Colón. Get checked in, get oriented, maybe grab a coffee — you've earned it.
7:30 PM
Flamenco Show at Tablao La Carmela
Dinner included. Near Puerta del Sol. This is the real thing — not a tourist gimmick. The venue is built into former coal cellars of a 19th century convent. The dancing will mess with you in the best possible way.
2 Wednesday, April 22 Madrid
Morning
Free Day — Explore Madrid
No scheduled tours today until evening. Good day for the Prado, El Retiro park, or just wandering. Madrid rewards wanderers.
7:00 PM
Madrid Tapas Adventure — Small Group Tour
~3.5 hours. This is how Spaniards actually eat — moving from bar to bar, a plate here, a glass there. Pay attention to where they take you because you'll want to go back on your own.
Free Morning · My Top Recommendation
The Prado

If you do one thing with your free time in Madrid, make it the Prado. This isn't just a great museum — it's one of the greatest collections of European painting on the planet, and it's yours for the morning.


What not to miss: Go straight to Velázquez. His painting Las Meninas (1656) is in Room 12 and it will stop you cold — it's a painting about looking at paintings, and the longer you stand in front of it the stranger and more interesting it gets. Don't rush past it. Also find Goya's Saturn Devouring His Son in the Goya rooms — it's brutal and unforgettable, painted directly on the wall of his own house when he was old, deaf, and done with humanity's nonsense. The Flemish collection upstairs has Hieronymus Bosch's The Garden of Earthly Delights, a triptych so bizarre and detailed you could spend an hour on it alone and still not catch everything.


Historical context worth knowing: The Prado opened in 1819 but the collection was built over centuries by Spanish royalty — which means what you're looking at is essentially the personal art collection of some of the most powerful rulers in Western history. Velázquez was the court painter to King Philip IV. Goya painted royalty too, but by the end of his life he was painting nightmares. That arc — from court flattery to Saturn — is one of the most interesting stories in art history, and you can see the whole thing in one building.


Practical: The Prado is at Paseo del Prado s/n — about a 15-minute walk from your hotel or a short taxi. Opens at 10 AM Tuesday through Sunday, closed Mondays. General admission is €15. Buy tickets online in advance at museodelprado.es — the line without a ticket can be long. Two to three hours is enough to see the highlights without your feet giving out on you.


→ See the Prado tab for images and deeper notes on each painting.

3 Thursday, April 23 Madrid
8:30 AM
Royal Palace Skip-the-Line Guided Tour
~5.5 hours. Madrid's historic center, then into the Royal Palace itself. 3,000 rooms — don't try to absorb all of it. Let the frescoed ceilings and the Royal Armory do the heavy lifting. The scale alone is worth the morning.
4 Friday, April 24 Madrid
9:15 AM
Ribera del Duero Winery Tour & Tasting
Full day — 11 hours. This is Spain's answer to Napa, except older and arguably more interesting. The Duero river valley has been making wine since before the Romans showed up. You'll taste why that matters.
🚄
Madrid → Seville by High-Speed Train
April 25 · Departs Puerta de Atocha 10:30 AM · Arrives Sevilla Santa Justa 1:13 PM · ~2h 43min · Confirmation: NEWRAIL
5 Saturday, April 25 Madrid → Seville
Morning
Private Transfer to Atocha Station
Pickup from Hotel Fenix Gran Meliá. Get there early — Atocha is gorgeous and worth a few minutes to look around before you board.
Afternoon
Arrive Seville · Check into Hospes Las Casas del Rey de Baeza
Hotel Address: Plaza Jesus De La Redencion, 2. You're now in one of the most beautiful cities in Europe. Give yourself time to just walk around the Santa Cruz neighborhood before dinner.
6 Sunday, April 26 Seville
10:30 AM
Spanish Cooking Class & Triana Market Tour
~3.5 hours. Meet at Triana Food Market, stalls 75–79, Plaza del Altozano. You'll cook a 4-course meal with a professional chef. Triana is the old gypsy quarter across the river — the market itself is worth the trip before class even starts.
7 Monday, April 27 Seville
TBD
Cathedral, Alcázar & Jewish Quarter Walking Tour
~3 hours. Note: Passport details required to confirm this tour — check with your travel agent. The Cathedral here is the largest Gothic cathedral in the world. The Alcázar is the real thing that Game of Thrones was pretending to be. The Jewish Quarter (Barrio Santa Cruz) will probably be your favorite neighborhood of the whole trip.
Evening
Last Night in Spain
No agenda. Seville's nights are long and warm in late April. Find a table outside and stay too late. You'll be fine.
🚄
Seville → Madrid by Train · Then Home
April 28 · Departs Sevilla Santa Justa 7:18 AM · Arrives Madrid Atocha 10:10 AM · Then private transfer to airport · Confirmation: NEWRAIL
8 Tuesday, April 28 Seville → Home
Early Morning
Departure Day
That 7:18 AM train is early. Set two alarms. Your private transfer from Hospes Las Casas will get you to Santa Justa station — make sure you're at the right station (Santa Justa, not any other Seville stop).

Pinch to zoom · Tap map buttons to switch views

Eating in Spain

The short version: eat late, eat slowly, and never apologize for ordering more bread.

🍽️ Tapas Etiquette

Tapas aren't an appetizer — they're a way of life. The point isn't to get full fast, it's to graze your way through a neighborhood over a couple of hours. Order one or two things, finish them, order more. Standing at the bar is completely normal and often preferred. Don't sit down expecting table service at a tapas bar — look around and do what the locals do.


One rule: If there are free tapas on the bar, they're free. In Madrid especially, some bars will drop a small plate of something when you order a drink. Eat it. That's the deal.

🕐 When Spaniards Actually Eat

Breakfast is small — coffee and a pastry, maybe a tostada. They eat it standing at a café counter around 9–10 AM.


Lunch is the main meal and it happens at 2–4 PM. Many restaurants won't even open for lunch until 2. If you're hungry at noon, you're on American time — have a tapa to tide you over.


Dinner doesn't really start until 9 PM. Showing up at 7 puts you in a nearly empty restaurant being served by a slightly bored waiter. Wait until at least 8:30, ideally 9. Trust the process.

💬 Useful Phrases at a Restaurant
Una mesa para dos, por favor.A table for two, please.
La carta, por favor.The menu, please.
¿Qué recomienda?What do you recommend?
Dos cervezas, por favor.Two beers, please.
Un vino tinto / blanco.A red / white wine.
Agua con gas / sin gas.Sparkling / still water.
¡Está delicioso!This is delicious!
La cuenta, por favor.The check, please.
¿Está incluido el servicio?Is service included?
💶 Tipping

Spain is not America. Tipping is appreciated but not expected in the way it is back home. At restaurants, rounding up or leaving a euro or two is perfectly fine — nobody is calculating 20%. At tapas bars, leaving your change is enough. Tour guides and private drivers do appreciate tips and it's more expected in those contexts — €5–10 per person for a half-day tour is reasonable. Don't overthink it.

🥘 Things Worth Ordering

In Madrid: Cocido madrileño (a hearty chickpea stew), huevos rotos (fried eggs broken over potatoes and jamón), and literally any jamón ibérico you see. The Jamón Ibérico de Bellota — from pigs that roam free eating acorns — is one of those things that's hard to explain until you've had it.


In Seville: Salmorejo (thicker, richer cousin of gazpacho), espinacas con garbanzos (spinach and chickpeas, sounds boring, isn't), and whatever fresh pescado (fish) is on the menu. Seville is close enough to the coast that the seafood is the real deal.

How Spain Works

A few things that will make the trip feel less like a vacation and more like you actually live there — at least for a week.

⏰ The Spanish Schedule

Spain runs about 2–3 hours later than the rest of Europe, and that's not an accident — it's a lifestyle. Shops often close from about 2–5 PM (the siesta, though younger Spaniards will tell you that's mostly gone in big cities, which is only half true). Museums tend to close one day a week, usually Monday. Churches often close midday. Build some flex into your plans.


The golden rule: If something looks closed, come back in an hour. If it's still closed, come back tomorrow morning.

🔊 Noise & Volume

Spanish social life is loud and that's considered normal and healthy. Don't be alarmed by the noise level in a restaurant or bar — everyone is talking over everyone and nobody is bothered.

👗 Dress

Spaniards dress well, especially in cities. You don't need to be formal, but athletic wear and flip-flops outside of a beach context will clock you as a tourist instantly — which is fine, but worth knowing. For cathedral visits, covered shoulders and knees are required. A light scarf solves this problem in about three seconds.

📱 Practical Stuff

Spain uses Type F plugs (two round pins). Bring a universal adapter or pick one up at the airport. Voltage is 220V — most modern devices handle this automatically, but check your chargers.


Currency: Euros. Cards are widely accepted but carry some cash for small tapas bars, markets, and anywhere with a handwritten sign in the window.


Bathrooms: Most cafés and bars expect you to buy something to use the restroom.

Why These Places Matter

The short version of a very long story.

👑 Madrid — The Accidental Capital

Madrid became Spain's capital essentially because King Philip II put his court there in 1561, and it stuck. Before that it was a minor Moorish fortress town called Magerit. The city grew up around the monarchy — which is why everything interesting here is either a palace, a church, or a museum that used to be one of those two things. The Prado alone holds more accumulated power and wealth than most countries.

🏛️ The Royal Palace

Royal Palace of Madrid

Built in the 18th century on the site of a former Moorish castle that burned down (conveniently, some historians note). Philip V wanted something that would make Versailles look modest — he nearly succeeded. It's the largest royal palace in Western Europe by floor area. The Spanish royal family doesn't actually live here anymore; they use a smaller palace outside the city. This one is basically too large and magnificent to actually live in.

💃 Flamenco — Not What You Think It Is

Flamenco didn't come from the aristocracy — it came from the margins. It emerged in Andalusia (southern Spain) in the 18th century from a collision of Romani, Moorish, Jewish, and Andalusian working-class cultures. It was the music of people who didn't have power, which is why it carries so much emotional weight. The word itself may come from the Spanish word for Flemish — there's a theory that "flamenco" was a slur applied to Romani people thought to look like Flemish immigrants. The origin is disputed. The art form isn't.

🕌 Seville & the Moors

The Real Alcázar, Seville — still an active royal residence

Seville was one of the most important cities in the Moorish empire — the Moors being the Muslim inhabitants of North Africa and the Iberian Peninsula, primarily of Arab and Berber descent — that controlled most of Spain from 711 to 1492 — a period called Al-Andalus. The Moors brought with them advances in mathematics, astronomy, agriculture, and architecture that Europe largely didn't have yet. The Alcázar you'll visit was a Moorish palace before it was a Christian one, and Christian kings liked it so much they kept building onto it in Moorish style even after the Reconquista. That style — Mudéjar — is why Seville looks like nowhere else in Europe.

⛵ Seville & the New World

Seville Cathedral — the largest Gothic cathedral in the world

Columbus sailed from near here in 1492 — the same year the Moors were expelled from Granada, which is not a coincidence. Spain suddenly had a lot of energy to redirect. For the next two centuries, Seville was the only port in Spain legally allowed to trade with the Americas. All the gold and silver and goods from the New World passed through this city. The wealth was staggering. The Cathedral you'll visit was built partly as a statement of that wealth — they wanted to build something so large, the quote goes, that "those who see it finished will think we were mad to have begun it."

🍷 Ribera del Duero — Wine Before the Romans

The Duero river valley has been producing wine for over 2,500 years. The Romans formalized it, the medieval monasteries kept it going through the dark ages (literally — monks needed wine for mass and apparently weren't too strict about quantities), and modern Spain turned it into one of the world's great wine regions. The Tempranillo grape grown here produces wines that can age for decades. The landscape — high plateau, cold winters, hot summers — stresses the vines in exactly the right way.

Getting Around

The practical stuff — so you spend your energy on Spain, not on figuring out Spain.

✈️ Arrival: Madrid Airport → Hotel

Your private transfer is already arranged. A driver will meet you at the airport with your name. Don't get into any other taxi or car that approaches you first — Madrid's airport has persistent unofficial drivers and they're not worth the hassle. Find your name on a sign and you're set.


Hotel Fenix Gran Meliá
Hermosilla 2, Madrid 28001
+34 914 316 700

🚇 Getting Around Madrid

Madrid's metro is excellent — clean, cheap, and covers the whole city. A 10-trip metro card (Tarjeta Multi) costs about €12 and is worth getting if you're going to use it more than a few times. That said, the historic center is very walkable. Most of your tour pickups will be at or near the hotel.


Taxis are metered and reliable. Ride apps like Cabify and Uber also work. Avoid flagging unmarked cars.

🚄 Madrid → Seville: The AVE Train

April 25 · Departs 10:30 AM · Arrives 1:13 PM
Train: IRYO 06106 · Confirmation: NEWRAIL


Your private transfer picks you up at Hotel Fenix and takes you to Madrid Puerta de Atocha station. Make sure you board from Atocha — not Chamartín, Madrid's other main station.


The AVE (Alta Velocidad Española) hits about 300 km/h. The ride through the Castilian plateau is genuinely beautiful. You'll arrive at Sevilla Santa Justa station where another private transfer takes you to the hotel.


Hospes Las Casas del Rey de Baeza
Plaza Jesus De La Redencion 2, Seville 41003
+34 954 561 496

🚶 Getting Around Seville

Seville's historic center is compact and best on foot. Your hotel is in the Santa Cruz neighborhood — the Cathedral, Alcázar, and the river are all within easy walking distance. The Triana market for your cooking class is across the river; it's about a 15-minute walk or a quick taxi.


Seville also has a good bus system and taxis are cheap by American standards. The city is flat, which makes it very bikeable if you're inclined.

🌅 Departure Day — April 28

This is an early morning. Your train departs Sevilla Santa Justa at 7:18 AM — set two alarms. Your private transfer leaves from Hospes Las Casas; make sure you confirm the pickup time with the hotel the night before.


Train: RENFE 2071 · Arrives Madrid Atocha 10:10 AM · Confirmation: NEWRAIL


From Atocha, another private transfer takes you directly to Madrid airport. Check your flight time and make sure the transfer timing works — if anything feels tight, call your travel agent (Mark Sherman, Cruise Planners) or the transfer company directly.


Reminder: Board and exit at the exact stations listed on your tickets. Both Madrid and Seville have more than one train station.

The Prado

Three paintings you need to stand in front of. Not read about — stand in front of.

Las Meninas
Diego Velázquez · 1656 · Room 12

This is the one that will stop you cold. A Spanish princess stands at the center of a large room, surrounded by her ladies-in-waiting, a dog, a dwarf, and various courtiers. Velázquez himself is in the painting, brush in hand, looking out at you. In the background, a mirror reflects what appears to be the king and queen — which means you, standing there looking at the painting, are standing where the king and queen stood. You're in the painting. It's a picture about looking at pictures, and the longer you stand in front of it the stranger and more interesting it gets. Art historians have been arguing about what it means for 350 years. Don't try to resolve it — just let it work on you.

Saturn Devouring His Son
Francisco Goya · c. 1819–1823 · Goya Rooms

Goya painted this directly on the wall of his own house when he was old, deaf, and had watched Napoleon's army tear Spain apart. He wasn't painting it for anyone — it was one of fourteen "Black Paintings" found on his walls after he died. Saturn, the Roman god who feared being overthrown by his children, is eating one of them. The expression on Saturn's face — wide-eyed, frantic, unstoppable — is one of the most unsettling things in Western art. What makes it stranger is that earlier in his career Goya was a celebrated court painter who did charming portraits of royalty. This is the same man, forty years later. That arc is worth knowing when you're standing in front of it.

The Garden of Earthly Delights
Hieronymus Bosch · c. 1490–1510 · Flemish Rooms

A triptych — three panels hinged together — painted around 1500 by a Flemish artist nobody fully understands. The left panel is the Garden of Eden. The right panel is Hell. The enormous middle panel is everything in between, which turns out to be a fever dream of naked figures, giant fruit, impossible creatures, and scenes that have no obvious explanation. Nobody knows exactly what it means. Scholars have argued it's a moral warning, a utopian fantasy, an alchemical allegory, or something else entirely. Here's the thing — you don't need to know what it means to be completely absorbed by it. Pick any square inch of the middle panel and just look. There's always something you didn't see before. Budget more time here than you think you need.
